perturbation.original_text: SHPS and HPS each agree that all information communicated to it by the other will be held in strict confidence and will be used only for purposes of this Agreement, and that no such information will be disclosed by the recipient party, its agents or employees without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: SHPS and HPS each agree that all information communicated to it by the other will be held in strict confidence and will be used only for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This clause removes the requirement for prior written consent before disclosing confidential information, potentially violating HIPAA regulations related to Protected Health Information (PHI).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA mandates specific safeguards for protecting the privacy of individually identifiable health information. By removing the requirement for prior written consent, the contract creates a loophole that could allow unauthorized disclosure of PHI, potentially violating HIPAA's privacy rule.

All covered entities, except "small health plans," must have been compliant with the Privacy Rule by April 14, 2003. 90 Small health plans, however, had until April 14, 2004 to comply. Small Health Plans. A health plan with annual receipts of not more than $5 million is a small health plan. 91 Health plans that file certain federal tax returns and report receipts on those returns should use the guidance provided by the Small Business Administration at 13 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 121.104 to calculate annual receipts. Health plans that do not report receipts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), for example, group health plans regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act 1974 (ERISA) that are exempt from filing income tax returns, should use proxy measures to determine their annual receipts. 92 See What constitutes a small health plan? perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for prior written consent before disclosing confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation states this could potentially violate HIPAA regulations related to Protected Health Information (PHI), as it could allow unauthorized disclosure of PHI. The Law Explanation supports this as well. Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally discusses uses and disclosures of protected health information, which are pertinent to HIPAA regulations.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either party materially or repeatedly defaults in the performance of any of its duties or obligations hereunder and does not substantially cure such default within thirty days after being given written notice specifying the default, or, with respect to those defaults which cannot reasonably be cured within thirty days, if the defaulting party fails to proceed promptly after being given such notice to commence curing the default and thereafter to reasonably proceed to cure the same, then the party not in default 2 may, by giving written notice to the defaulting party, terminate this Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either party materially or repeatedly defaults in the performance of any of its duties or obligations hereunder and does not substantially cure such default, then the party not in default 2 may, by giving written notice to the defaulting party, terminate this Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the cure period for defaults, potentially violating Florida Statute 672.709, which allows sellers the right to cure a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the cure period contradicts Florida Statute 672.709 which, as part of Florida's adoption of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), generally allows a seller (in this case, potentially HPS or SHPS depending on the nature of the default) the right to cure a breach of contract. Eliminating this right to cure creates a contractual provision that is less favorable to the parties than what is provided for under Florida law.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 709 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 709 Action for the price. F.S. 672.709 672.709 Action for the price. — (1) When the buyer fails to pay the price as it becomes due the seller may recover, together with any incidental damages under the next section, the price: (a) Of goods accepted or of conforming goods lost or damaged within a commercially reasonable time after risk of their loss has passed to the buyer; and (b) Of goods identified to the contract if the seller is unable after reasonable effort to resell them at a reasonable price or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing. (2) Where the seller sues for the price she or he must hold for the buyer any goods which have been identified to the contract and are still in her or his control except that if resale becomes possible the seller may resell them at any time prior to the collection of the judgment. The net proceeds of any such resale must be credited to the buyer and payment of the judgment entitles her or him to any goods not resold. (3) After the buyer has wrongfully rejected or revoked acceptance of the goods or has failed to make a payment due or has repudiated (s. 672.610 ), a seller who is held not entitled to the price under this section shall nevertheless be awarded damages for nonacceptance under the preceding section. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 606, ch. 97-102. Note. — s. 2-709, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text provides a 30-day cure period for defaults, whereas the changed text eliminates this cure period entirely. The Perturbation Explanation states that this removal potentially violates Florida Statute 672.709, which allows sellers the right to cure a breach. The Law Explanation supports this, noting that removing the cure period contradicts Florida Statute 672.709 and creates a contractual provision less favorable than what is provided under Florida law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Florida Statute 672.709, concerning action for the price, and while it doesn't explicitly mention the right to cure, the explanation strongly implies that this statute encompasses such a right in sales contracts. The omission of the cure period is a significant departure from the potential rights afforded under Florida law, making the modified contract less favorable and potentially unenforceable in certain scenarios.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws, other than choice of law rules, of the state of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Florida.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'other than choice of law rules' may be problematic, particularly if the services are provided in multiple states. It could unintentionally violate consumer protection laws of other states where the client agreements are based, like California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the exception for 'choice of law rules', the contract may inadvertently subject all disputes to Florida law, even if a Client Agreement is governed by California law and its consumer protection statutes. This could limit a California consumer's rights under the CLRA, as they might be forced to pursue claims under Florida law, which may be less favorable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'other than choice of law rules' directly contradicts consumer protection laws like California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA), as indicated by the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 'CIV 1750' which can be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act, strengthens the reasoning that the omission could potentially limit a California consumer's rights, as they might be forced to pursue claims under Florida law. The changed text removes a provision that would have allowed the application of other states' laws, thereby undermining consumer protection mandates by potentially subjecting all disputes to Florida law, even when a Client Agreement is governed by another state's consumer protection statutes.
